trm:term.trm:name: Specification Connectivity
trm:term.trm:definition: A graph-theoretic measure of how well a specification's content nodes are linked through the relation layer. A fully connected specification has one weakly connected component and zero isolated nodes, meaning every concept is reachable from every other concept via semantic links. Fragmented specifications have disconnected clusters or orphaned nodes that are not part of any relation.

trm:term.trm:name: Reference Synthesis
trm:term.trm:definition: The automatic generation of rel:relation type="references" edges from trm:ref elements in the prose layer. Every trm:ref that cites a term creates an implicit semantic link from the nearest ancestor node (the section or term containing the reference) to the cited term. These implicit links are made explicit by an XSLT post-processing step applied to the combined document, so that the relation layer reflects the actual usage patterns in the prose without requiring authors to hand-write reference relations.

trm:term.trm:name: Acyclic Relation
trm:term.trm:definition: A relation type whose edges must form a directed acyclic graph (DAG). Cycles involving acyclic relation types are specification errors because they violate the inherent semantics of the relation. Two of the seven relation kinds are acyclic: depends-on (a dependency cycle means nothing can be built first) and precedes (a temporal ordering cycle means nothing can happen first). The remaining five kinds (refines, conflicts-with, implements, constrains, reverses) may legitimately participate in cycles without indicating an error.

trm:term.trm:name: Extension Relation Type
trm:term.trm:definition: A relation type defined by an extension layer rather than the core RelationKind enumeration. Extension types are XSD complexTypes annotated with rel:relation-type-properties in xs:appinfo, activated by a refines edge from their type id to type-relation-kind. The tooling discovers them via two-pass collection and treats their instances as graph edges using the declared attribute names for source and target node IDs.

pr:section.pr:title: Connectivity Analysis
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How the CLI tool analyzes the relation graph to measure specification cohesion.
pr:section.pr:p: The connectivity command builds a directed graph from the specification's content nodes and semantic relations, then reports connectivity metrics. The graph excludes metadata nodes (artifact mappings and revisions) because they are administrative, not conceptual.

Graph construction collects all elements with @id attributes from all layer files and from all .xsd schema files (making XSD types with @id attributes addressable graph nodes). It filters out art:mapping and rev:revision elements, and adds each remaining node as a vertex. Relations are collected via a two-pass process: pass 1 gathers explicit rel:relation elements, pass 2 discovers and collects instances of extension relation types (see extensible relation types). Each relation becomes a directed edge, provided both endpoints exist in the graph and the relation is local (cross-spec relations with a to-spec attribute are skipped).

pr:ul.pr:li: Summary: node count, edge count, and graph density.
Relation type distribution: count of each relation kind (depends-on, precedes, refines, etc.).
Connected components: number of weakly connected components, with per-component detail when fragmented.
Isolated nodes: nodes with zero in-degree and zero out-degree, indicating completely disconnected content.
Hub nodes: top 5 nodes by total degree, revealing the most connected concepts.
Bridge nodes: top 5 by betweenness centrality, identifying nodes whose removal would fragment the graph.
Cycles: simple cycles in the directed graph, which may indicate circular dependencies.

pr:section.pr:p: The command exits with code 0 when the specification is fully connected (one component, zero isolated nodes) and code 1 when fragmentation is detected. This enables CI/CD integration for connectivity enforcement.

pr:section.pr:title: Acyclic Relation Cycle Detection
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How the connectivity tool classifies cycles as errors or benign based on relation type semantics.
pr:section.pr:p: Not all cycles are equal. The seven relation kinds fall into two categories based on whether cycles are semantically valid:

pr:tr.pr:td: Error: circular dependency or impossible temporal ordering
Benign: mutual refinement, bidirectional constraints, etc.

pr:section.pr:p: The connectivity tool detects all simple cycles in the directed graph, then inspects the relation types along each cycle's edges. If any edge in a cycle uses an acyclic relation type, the cycle is flagged as an error requiring review. Cycles composed entirely of cyclic-tolerant types are reported without an error marker.

A depends-on cycle (A depends-on B depends-on A) means neither node can be understood or built without the other already existing. A precedes cycle (X precedes Y precedes X) means neither event can happen first. Both are logical contradictions in the specification that must be resolved by removing or redirecting one of the relations.
pr:section.pr:note: Mixed-type cycles (e.g., A depends-on B, B refines A) are also flagged as errors if any edge is acyclic. The acyclic constraint applies per-edge, not per-cycle: a single depends-on or precedes edge in a cycle is sufficient to flag it.

pr:section.pr:title: Reference Synthesis via XSLT
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How trm:ref elements in prose become rel:relation edges in the relation layer.
pr:section.pr:p: When an author writes <trm:ref term="some-term"> in prose, they are making a semantic statement: this section uses or cites that term. This is the same relationship that rel:relation type="references" expresses in the relation layer. Rather than forcing authors to maintain both the inline reference and a separate relation element, reference synthesis derives the relation automatically.

The synthesis happens during combined document assembly. After all layer files are merged into a single document, an XSLT 1.0 post-processing transform (postprocess.xslt) scans the combined tree for trm:ref elements. For each unique (ancestor-id, term) pair, the transform emits a rel:relation type="references" from="{ancestor-id}" to="{term}" element. The ancestor is the nearest enclosing element with an @id attribute, which is typically a section or term definition. Self-references (where the ancestor is the cited term itself) are skipped.

The XSLT uses Muenchian grouping to deduplicate: if a section references the same term three times, only one relation edge is emitted. The transform is an identity transform that copies the entire document unchanged and appends the synthesized relations at the root, so existing content and explicit relations are preserved.

The Python tooling applies this transform via lxml (which provides XSLT 1.0 support through libxslt). Both assemble_combined() (used by validation) and _collect_relations() (used by the connectivity command) go through the same XSLT pipeline, ensuring that validation and connectivity analysis see identical relation graphs.

org:task.org:purpose: The CLI tool analyzes the relation graph to measure specification cohesion, identifying fragmentation, hubs, bridges, and cycles.
The XSLT post-processing step synthesizes relation edges from terminology references, bridging the prose and relation layers without manual duplication.

org:reference.org:purpose: Reference material on which relation types are acyclic, how cycles are classified, and what constitutes an error.

rel:relation.rel:note: Connectivity analysis depends on the specification connectivity concept for its semantic foundation.
Connectivity analysis depends on the layered architecture for understanding which nodes are content and which are metadata.
The reference synthesis section depends on the reference synthesis concept for its semantic foundation.
Reference synthesis depends on trm:ref being a cross-layer reference mechanism: the prose layer's term references become the input that the XSLT transforms into relation-layer edges.
Connectivity analysis depends on reference synthesis to connect terminology nodes that would otherwise be isolated in the graph.
The cycle detection section depends on the acyclic relation concept for its classification rules.
Connectivity analysis depends on the extension relation type concept for its two-pass collection process.
Acyclic cycle detection is a sub-feature of the connectivity analysis command.

art:mapping.art:note: analyze_connectivity() main function: graph construction, schema discovery, and orchestration of the analysis pipeline.
Relation struct, collect_nodes_and_relations: extract typed relations from combined document.
collect_from_tree: recursive XML tree traversal to extract nodes, relations, and acyclic type references.
RelationType complex type defines the structure that connectivity analysis consumes: type, from, to attributes for graph edges, plus to-spec/to-revision for cross-spec filtering.
_build_spec_graph() implements the connectivity concept by building a directed graph from content nodes and relation edges.
RelationKind simpleType with rel:acyclic annotations on each enumeration value defines which relation types must form DAGs.
postprocess.xslt: XSLT 1.0 identity transform + Muenchian grouping that synthesizes rel:relation type="references" from trm:ref elements.
_apply_reference_synthesis() and assemble_combined() (252-296) apply the XSLT via lxml during combined document assembly.
The postprocess.xslt transform is the canonical implementation of the reference synthesis concept.

discover_extension_relation_types() (389-451) implements the annotation scanning that defines extension types. _collect_relations_extended() (454-519) implements the two-pass discovery and collection process.

spec:clayers.llm:node: The connectivity command builds a directed graph from spec nodes and relations using NetworkX, then reports: summary (nodes, edges, density), relation type distribution, connected components, isolated nodes, hub nodes (top 5 by degree), bridge nodes (top 5 by betweenness centrality), and cycles. Exit code 0 means fully connected, 1 means fragmented. Metadata nodes (art:mapping, rev:revision) are excluded from the graph. Cross-spec relations are skipped.

Specification connectivity measures how well content nodes are linked through semantic relations. A fully connected spec has one weakly connected component and zero isolated nodes. The connectivity command uses NetworkX graph algorithms to compute this.

The connectivity tool classifies each detected cycle by inspecting edge relation types. depends-on and precedes are acyclic (must form DAGs); cycles containing these types are flagged as errors. The other five relation kinds (refines, conflicts-with, implements, constrains, reverses) tolerate cycles. Mixed-type cycles are flagged if any edge is acyclic. The acyclicity constraint is annotated in relation.xsd via rel:acyclic appinfo on the RelationKind enumeration values.

The postprocess.xslt transform scans the combined document for trm:ref elements and emits one rel:relation type="references" per unique (ancestor-id, term) pair. The ancestor is the nearest element with an @id attribute. Muenchian grouping deduplicates. Self-references are skipped. The transform is applied via lxml by both assemble_combined() (validation path) and _collect_relations() (connectivity path), so both see the same synthesized edges.

Reference synthesis automatically generates rel:relation type="references" edges from trm:ref elements in prose. This bridges the prose and relation layers: authors write inline term references, and the tooling derives the corresponding semantic links via XSLT post-processing. Without synthesis, terminology nodes would appear isolated in the connectivity graph despite being actively used in prose.

A relation type defined outside the core RelationKind enum. Extension layers create XSD complexTypes with rel:relation-type-properties annotations declaring relation-name, acyclicity, and from/to attribute names. A refines edge to type-relation-kind activates the type. The two-pass connectivity collection discovers these types in pass 1 (via the refines edge) and collects their instances in pass 2.

An acyclic relation type must form a DAG: cycles are specification errors. depends-on cycles mean circular dependencies; precedes cycles mean impossible temporal ordering. The remaining five relation kinds tolerate cycles. The relation.xsd schema annotates each enumeration value with rel:acyclic="true" or rel:acyclic="false".
